title: Genesis 26
subtitle: Isaac in Gerar: the Covenant Continued

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jacob and Esau Born

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — Isaac in Gerar; the Covenant Reaffirmed

poi.name: Gerar / Philistine territory
poi.text: Isaac goes to Gerar — the same Philistine city where Abraham used the wife-sister deception. The covenant pattern recurs in the same place. Abimelech's name suggests either the same king as Abraham's encounter or a dynastic title.

notes.note: MacArthur: God appears to Isaac in Gerar and commands him to stay -- not to go to Egypt as Abraham had done. The Abrahamic covenant is formally reaffirmed to Isaac for the first time: the same land, seed, and blessing promises. The explicit ground: 'because Abraham obeyed me and did everything I required' (v.5). Parental covenant faithfulness benefits subsequent generations.

MacArthur: Isaac's wife-sister deception in Gerar is a near-verbatim repetition of Abraham's deception -- the same location, the same king, the same failure. The sin pattern is transmitted generationally. Abimelech's rebuke (v.9-10) is essentially the same as his predecessor's of Abraham. The covenant family's recurring failure requires recurring divine intervention.

heb.paragraph: God's instruction to Isaac — "Do not go down to Egypt; live in the land where I tell you to stay" (v.2) — uses the directional verbs that pattern the patriarchal narrative. Egypt is always "down" (moral and physical descent); Canaan is always "up" (covenant ascent). The prohibition on going to Egypt prevents Isaac from repeating Abraham's Egypt-sojourn and keeps him within the covenant land through the famine. The covenant line is tested in the land, not protected from it by going elsewhere.

"Stay in this land for a while, and I will be with you (ʾehyeh ʿimmĕkā) and will bless you" (v.3) — the divine promise ʾehyeh ʿimmĕkā ("I will be/I am with you") is the covenantal presence-formula that sustains the patriarchs and ultimately becomes the divine name revealed at Sinai (Exod 3:14: "I AM who I AM" — ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh). The promise of divine presence is the content of the covenant blessing — what makes "I will bless you" real is "I will be with you."

"I will make your descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky... because (ʿēqeb ʾăšer) Abraham obeyed me and kept my requirements" (v.4-5). The ʿēqeb ("because/in the consequence of") grounds Isaac's covenant blessing in Abraham's prior obedience — the covenant passes through Abraham's faithfulness to his descendants. This is not merit-theology but covenantal solidarity: the covenant community shares in the consequences of its representative's obedience, just as it will share in the consequences of Adam's disobedience (Rom 5:12-21).

hist.context: The covenant renewal to Isaac is structurally parallel to the original call of Abraham — famine triggers migration, God intervenes with the promise, the patriarch obeys. The repetition is not redundancy but theological insistence: the covenant is not inherited automatically. Each generation must receive it afresh. God appears to Isaac directly, confirming that the relationship is personal, not merely dynastic.

refs.note: The original covenant to Abraham — Isaac now receives the same three elements: land, offspring, and universal blessing.
The blessing through the offspring "because you obeyed" — now explicitly cited as the reason Isaac inherits the same promise.
Isaac "by faith blessed Jacob and Esau" — his role in the covenant story is that of the middle link in the chain of promise.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: God appears to Isaac during a famine and renews the Abrahamic covenant explicitly. Sarna: "This is the only extended divine speech addressed to Isaac in Genesis. The promise is articulated in the same terms as the Abraham covenant (land, descendants, universal blessing) and is grounded explicitly in Abraham's obedience — 'because Abraham obeyed me and kept my requirements.' The covenant passes through obedience, not mere biological descent."

The third wife-sister episode — Isaac claiming Rebekah as his sister — is a repetition of Abraham's pattern. Sarna: "The repetition is both realistic (the strategy worked before; why not again?) and theological: the covenant heir inherits not only the promises but the failures. The pattern of human compromise within divine covenant faithfulness characterises the entire patriarchal narrative."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: A famine in the land. The same opening phrase appeared in 12:10, sending Abraham to Egypt. Alter: "The famine tests whether Isaac will repeat his father's pattern of seeking human solutions (Egypt) or trust the divine word. God intervenes before Isaac departs, directing him to stay in Canaan. The test Isaac passes by obedience is the test Abraham failed — or, rather, the test God prevents Isaac from even reaching."

The covenant renewal formula is repeated almost verbatim from the Abraham episodes. Alter notes the intentional repetition: Isaac is not receiving a new covenant but the continuation of his father's covenant.

God appears to forbid the Egyptian escape. Calvin: "God permits no occasion to the faithful of forgetting his promises. Isaac was about to go down to Egypt; God says 'stay in this land, I will be with you.' The promise is renewed precisely at the point of temptation to abandon it. This is the divine pastoral care — not allowing his people to stray far before calling them back."

Calvin: The covenant rested on free grace, not Abraham's obedience. But faithful obedience is the means by which the covenant's benefits are actually received in history. The promise is unconditional; the enjoyment of it is shaped by faithfulness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "Abraham obeyed me and did everything I required of him" discusses whether this introduces a works-based element into the Abrahamic covenant. The note observes that the covenant was given unconditionally (15:17-18) but that Abraham's obedience is cited here as the occasion for its renewal to Isaac — consistent with the pattern in Genesis 22 where the Akedah triggers the divine oath.
The NET note observes this appearance occurs during a potentially dangerous stay with foreigners — the covenant promise is given precisely when the covenant holder needs reassurance most.

sections.header: Verses 12–22 — Isaac Prospers; the Wells of Contention

poi.name: Wells of the Negev / Beersheba valley
poi.text: Esek (contention), Sitnah (opposition), Rehoboth (room) — the well names trace Isaac's progress from conflict to space. The final well is undisputed. Each name preserves the history of the encounter: the geography of the Negev becomes a record of divine provision through conflict.

notes.note: MacArthur: The wells of Esek (contention), Sitnah (opposition), and Rehoboth (room) trace Isaac's response to conflict: he re-digs, encounters opposition, moves on, and eventually finds a place where there is room. The progressive withdrawal is not weakness but wisdom -- and God opens the space that fighting could not have secured.

MacArthur: 'Now the LORD has given us room and we will flourish in the land' -- Isaac interprets the opened well as covenant provision. The name Rehoboth (broad places/room) is Isaac's theological commentary on the event. God's method of blessing is to open space, not to win contests. The covenant community's posture is to receive what God opens, not to seize what opposition withholds.

heb.paragraph: "Isaac planted crops in that land and the same year reaped a hundredfold (meʾāh šĕʿārîm), because the LORD blessed him" (v.12). The hundredfold yield is extraordinary — typical yields in Palestinian agriculture were tenfold at best. The surplus is the visible, material form of covenant blessing: the prosperity is so unmistakable that "the Philistines envied him" (v.14) and Abimelech requests that he leave "because you have become too powerful for us." Covenant faithfulness produces a flourishing that the surrounding world cannot ignore.

The well-disputes of vv.19-22 (Esek — quarrel; Sitnah — enmity; Rehoboth — broad places) trace Isaac's progressive movement away from Philistine territory toward the space where God will reaffirm the covenant (v.24). Water in a semi-arid land is life itself — the wells are not merely economic assets but covenant survival markers. Each well dispute is a choice between standing one's ground and moving on; Isaac consistently moves on, and God responds with the covenant reaffirmation he needs.

"He named it Rehoboth, saying, 'Now the LORD has given us room (rāḥab) and we will flourish in the land'" (v.22). The name Rehoboth (from rāḥab — spacious) expresses the covenant theological principle: God makes space for his people when they respond to opposition with trust rather than resistance. The same root describes the covenant promise in Exod 3:8 — a land "large (rĕḥābāh) and good." The covenant promises spaciousness as the fruit of patient faith.

hist.context: Isaac's wife-as-sister deception is the third iteration of this pattern in Genesis (cf. 12:10–20; 20:1–18). The deliberate repetition invites comparison: Abraham lied twice; now Isaac lies once. The verb used for Isaac and Rebekah — "laughing" (meṣaḥēq) — is the piel of the same root as Isaac's name. The very name "Laughter" catches the patriarch in his lie. Abimelech again proves more righteous than the covenant man.

refs.note: The two Abrahamic precedents. The pattern's third occurrence suggests a structural theme: the covenant patriarch's fear vs. trust in God's protection.
The theological answer to the patriarch's recurring fear: "If God is for us, who can be against us?" — the promise of divine protection that the patriarchs grasped imperfectly.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Isaac's agricultural success — "a hundredfold" in the same year — is a direct fulfilment of the covenant blessing. Sarna: "The Philistine envious response — 'you have become too powerful for us' — is the first instance in Isaac's narrative of the blessing creating hostility. The same pattern will appear in Egypt (Exod 1:9). The covenant people's prosperity provokes the envy of their neighbours, a recurring theme in the patriarchal and Exodus narratives."

Isaac re-digs Abraham's wells and names the new wells Esek (contention), Sitnah (opposition), and Rehoboth (room). Sarna: "The well-digging narrative is more than a pastoral dispute — it is a progressive claim on Canaan. Each well is an act of habitation; the contentions are the resistance of the current occupants; the final name Rehoboth ('room') marks the moment when the land begins to yield to the covenant people."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The progression of well names — contention, opposition, room — traces a narrative arc of gradual establishment. Alter: "Isaac does not fight for the first two wells; he simply moves away and digs again. The patience is not cowardice but the posture of a man whose security does not depend on any particular piece of land but on the divine promise that undergirds his presence in Canaan."
The well-dispute sequence traces Isaac's movement from conflict toward the open land God provides. Alter notes each well's name is a station on a journey from human hostility to divine provision.

The hundredfold yield is a divine sign of covenant faithfulness even in the land of the Philistines. Calvin: "God blessed Isaac not according to his desert but according to his covenant with Abraham. The blessing came even in a foreign country, under the suspicion and envy of strangers — to show that the promise of God is not confined to any particular location."
Calvin: Isaac trusted God to give him room rather than seizing it by force. The man who fights for space often loses it; the man who waits for God to provide it receives more than he fought for.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Isaac re-digging Abraham's wells observes that water rights were the most contested resource in the ancient Near East. The note connects the well-disputes to the broader theme of land-claim in the patriarchal narratives: each well is a small assertion of presence in the promised land.

The NET note on the hundredfold harvest observes that the harvest made Isaac 'great' (gadol) — the same word applied to Abraham's greatness in 12:2. The covenant blessing is materially expressed as it was in the patriarch's generation.

sections.header: Verses 23–35 — The Lord Appears at Beersheba; Treaty with Abimelek

poi.name: Beersheba
poi.coords: Southern anchor of the Promised Land
poi.text: The LORD appears to Isaac at Beersheba — the same site where Abraham made his treaty. Isaac builds an altar here and digs a well. Beersheba becomes the double-covenant site: Abraham's treaty and now Isaac's theophany both occur here. The city will later mark the southern boundary of Israel.

notes.note: MacArthur: The LORD appeared to Isaac at Beersheba and reaffirmed the Abrahamic covenant: 'I am the God of your father Abraham.' The divine self-identification grounds the current promise in the prior covenant. Isaac built an altar and called on the name of the LORD -- worship precedes all other activities at the significant covenant sites.

MacArthur: Abimelech's treaty proposal acknowledges what the pagan king can observe from outside the covenant: 'We saw clearly that the LORD was with you.' The covenant presence of God with Isaac is perceptible to those who watch from outside. The visible blessing of the covenant community is a witness to the nations -- the fulfilment of the 'blessing to all peoples' promise has this dimension.

heb.paragraph: The divine reaffirmation at Beersheba — "I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid, for I am with you; I will bless you and will increase the number of your descendants" (v.24) — uses the standard covenant-renewal formula. The divine self-identification (ʾĕlōhê ʾābîkā ʾabrāhām) grounds the present promise in the past covenant. Isaac is not receiving a new promise but the continuation of the one already given to Abraham. The covenant is not re-negotiated at each generation but renewed and amplified.

Abimelech's treaty request — "Let there be a sworn agreement between us... a treaty of peace (ûniktĕrāh berît ʿimmāk)" (v.28) — recognises that Isaac's prosperity is covenant-sourced: "We saw clearly that the LORD was with you" (v.28). The non-covenant king seeks peace (šālôm — covenant wholeness) with the one blessed by God. The covenant blessing generates a šālôm that extends beyond Israel's borders — the fulfilment beginning of the universal blessing promised in 12:3.

Esau's marriages to Hittite women "were a source of grief (mōrat rûaḥ — bitterness of spirit) to Isaac and Rebekah" (v.35). The term mōrat rûaḥ ("bitterness of spirit") uses the same root as Marah (bitter water) and Naomi's "call me Māraʾ." The Hittite wives represent Esau's practical disregard for the covenant line's endogamy — the requirement that covenant descendants marry within the family rather than among the Canaanites. It is this grief that drives Rebekah's plan for Jacob in ch.27.

hist.context: Isaac re-digging Abraham's wells is an act of both practical necessity and theological continuity. The wells are covenant markers — stopping them was an act of erasure. Isaac's restoration of the wells, using Abraham's original names, is a claim of covenant continuity. Each well dispute (Esek = contention, Sitnah = accusation, Rehoboth = room) traces a spiritual geography of persecution → persistence → provision.

refs.note: Abraham made a similar treaty with Abimelek at Beersheba in chapter 21. Isaac's treaty at the same location with the same ruling family shows covenant relationships persisting across generations.

God's appearance at Beersheba (v. 24) prepares for the even greater theophany Jacob will receive at Bethel. The divine promises accumulate: each patriarch receives a personal confirmation of the Abrahamic covenant.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: God appears to Isaac at Beersheba with the same covenant renewal given to Abraham in 22:15-18. Sarna: "The appearance at Beersheba establishes this as the principal sacred site of the Isaac narrative. Isaac builds an altar, pitches his tent, and digs a well — the same three acts Abraham performed at each stage of his journey through Canaan. The pattern of worship-dwelling-resource marks the covenant people's presence in the land."

Abimelech comes to make a treaty with Isaac — the same pattern as chapter 21. Sarna: "The recognition by the Philistine king — 'we saw clearly that the LORD was with you' — is the external confirmation of the covenant blessing. Isaac's prosperity has been so evident that the regional power seeks a formal peace. The covenant people's blessing is visible to their neighbours; it creates both envy and respect."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abimelech comes to Isaac: "We saw clearly that the LORD was with you." Alter: "The pagan testimony continues the pattern of chapters 21 and 39 (Potiphar) and further — the divine favour on the covenant-bearer is unmistakably visible to outsiders. This is the covenant blessing operating in its widest sense: 'I will bless those who bless you.' Abimelech comes to make peace precisely because he has experienced the truth of this."

Abimelech's claim 'we saw clearly that the LORD was with you' is the covenant's external validation. Alter notes this is Genesis's pattern: the outsider most clearly articulates the insider's calling. The nations recognise what the covenant people sometimes take for granted.

God appears at Beersheba: "I am the God of your father Abraham; do not be afraid, for I am with you." Calvin: "The promise is given exactly when it is most needed — Isaac is in a new place, surrounded by contentious neighbours. 'Do not be afraid' is the divine word to every person who ventures in obedience into unknown territory. The presence of God is the answer to every fear."
Calvin: Isaac showed that the meekness which had submitted to injustice was not weakness — it was the patience of a man who knew God would eventually vindicate him without requiring him to fight.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "we saw clearly that the LORD was with you" observes that the divine name (YHWH) is used by the pagan king — an acknowledgment of the specific God of the Hebrews. The note discusses whether this implies genuine monotheistic belief by Abimelech or simply the recognition of a powerful patron deity.
The NET note on the oath by the LORD that Abimelech invokes observes: a pagan king swears by the covenant God of Israel. This is the 'blessing to all nations' (12:3) working itself out in practice.

ppl.name: Isaac
ppl.role: Covenant's second link; passive but faithful
ppl.text: The only patriarch with no major moral failing — his deception is the least harmful of the three sister-wife episodes, and he is caught rather than confronted. His re-digging of Abraham's wells and his Beersheba altar show genuine covenant continuity.

ppl.name: Rebekah
ppl.role: Matriarch; largely silent in this chapter
ppl.text: She is the object of the deception and the cause of its unraveling — "Isaac laughing with Rebekah" (v.8). Her character will emerge forcefully in the next chapter.

ppl.name: Abimelech
ppl.role: King of Gerar; recurring Philistine figure
ppl.text: Whether this is the same Abimelech as in ch.20–21 is debated — the name may be a royal title rather than a personal name. His moral clarity again exceeds the covenant man's. His confession ("the LORD has been with you") is the chapter's theological verdict.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"He moved on from there and dug another well, and no one quarreled over it. He named it Rehoboth, saying, 'Now the LORD has given us room and we will flourish in the land.'"ESV"He moved from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it. So he called its name Rehoboth, saying, 'For now the LORD has made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land.'"KJV"And he removed from thence, and digged another well; and for that they strove not: and he called the name of it Rehoboth; and he said, For now the LORD hath made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land."NASB"He moved away from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it; so he named it Rehoboth, for he said, 'At last the LORD has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in the land.'"

src.title: Abimelech as throne title
src.quote: Semitic scholars note that "Abimelech" (ʾāḇî + melek = "my father is king") functions as a Philistine royal title throughout Genesis, rather than a personal name. This explains why the same name appears across multiple generations. Similar ANE examples: "Pharaoh" as a title, not a personal name.

rec.text: Calvin used Isaac's hundredfold harvest as a key proof text for divine providence in economic life: God blesses the labor of the faithful, not because of merit but as covenant faithfulness made visible. He warned against the opposite error of attributing all prosperity to skill.

Commentators from Chrysostom onward have noted Isaac's peculiar role: he is the least developed patriarch, yet the most essential link. Without Isaac, the Abraham → Jacob chain breaks. His very passivity is a theological statement: the covenant's survival does not depend on heroic individuals.

rows.label: The Covenant Renewed: Obedience and Transmission
rows.text: God appears to Isaac in Gerar. The Abrahamic covenant is explicitly transmitted because Abraham obeyed -- the obedience is retrospective (Abraham's) and the blessing is prospective (Isaac's).

rows.label: Well Names: Conflict to Space
rows.text: Esek (contention), Sitnah (opposition), Rehoboth (room): the progression from dispute to withdrawal to the space God provides is a character arc in three place names.

hebtext.note: God's instruction to Isaac — "Do not go down to Egypt; live in the land where I tell you to stay" (v.2) — uses the directional verbs that pattern the patriarchal narrative. Egypt is always "down" (moral and physical descent); Canaan is always "up" (covenant ascent). The prohibition on going to Egypt prevents Isaac from repeating Abraham's Egypt-sojourn and keeps him within the covenant land through the famine. The covenant line is tested in the land, not protected from it by going elsewhere.

"Stay in this land for a while, and I will be with you (ʾehyeh ʿimmĕkā) and will bless you" (v.3) — the divine promise ʾehyeh ʿimmĕkā ("I will be/I am with you") is the covenantal presence-formula that sustains the patriarchs and ultimately becomes the divine name revealed at Sinai (Exod 3:14: "I AM who I AM" — ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh). The promise of divine presence is the content of the covenant blessing — what makes "I will bless you" real is "I will be with you."

"I will make your descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky... because (ʿēqeb ʾăšer) Abraham obeyed me and kept my requirements" (v.4-5). The ʿēqeb ("because/in the consequence of") grounds Isaac's covenant blessing in Abraham's prior obedience — the covenant passes through Abraham's faithfulness to his descendants. This is not merit-theology but covenantal solidarity: the covenant community shares in the consequences of its representative's obedience, just as it will share in the consequences of Adam's disobedience (Rom 5:12-21).

"Isaac planted crops in that land and the same year reaped a hundredfold (meʾāh šĕʿārîm), because the LORD blessed him" (v.12). The hundredfold yield is extraordinary — typical yields in Palestinian agriculture were tenfold at best. The surplus is the visible, material form of covenant blessing: the prosperity is so unmistakable that "the Philistines envied him" (v.14) and Abimelech requests that he leave "because you have become too powerful for us." Covenant faithfulness produces a flourishing that the surrounding world cannot ignore.

The well-disputes of vv.19-22 (Esek — quarrel; Sitnah — enmity; Rehoboth — broad places) trace Isaac's progressive movement away from Philistine territory toward the space where God will reaffirm the covenant (v.24). Water in a semi-arid land is life itself — the wells are not merely economic assets but covenant survival markers. Each well dispute is a choice between standing one's ground and moving on; Isaac consistently moves on, and God responds with the covenant reaffirmation he needs.

"He named it Rehoboth, saying, 'Now the LORD has given us room (rāḥab) and we will flourish in the land'" (v.22). The name Rehoboth (from rāḥab — spacious) expresses the covenant theological principle: God makes space for his people when they respond to opposition with trust rather than resistance. The same root describes the covenant promise in Exod 3:8 — a land "large (rĕḥābāh) and good." The covenant promises spaciousness as the fruit of patient faith.

The divine reaffirmation at Beersheba — "I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid, for I am with you; I will bless you and will increase the number of your descendants" (v.24) — uses the standard covenant-renewal formula. The divine self-identification (ʾĕlōhê ʾābîkā ʾabrāhām) grounds the present promise in the past covenant. Isaac is not receiving a new promise but the continuation of the one already given to Abraham. The covenant is not re-negotiated at each generation but renewed and amplified.

Abimelech's treaty request — "Let there be a sworn agreement between us... a treaty of peace (ûniktĕrāh berît ʿimmāk)" (v.28) — recognises that Isaac's prosperity is covenant-sourced: "We saw clearly that the LORD was with you" (v.28). The non-covenant king seeks peace (šālôm — covenant wholeness) with the one blessed by God. The covenant blessing generates a šālôm that extends beyond Israel's borders — the fulfilment beginning of the universal blessing promised in 12:3.

Esau's marriages to Hittite women "were a source of grief (mōrat rûaḥ — bitterness of spirit) to Isaac and Rebekah" (v.35). The term mōrat rûaḥ ("bitterness of spirit") uses the same root as Marah (bitter water) and Naomi's "call me Māraʾ." The Hittite wives represent Esau's practical disregard for the covenant line's endogamy — the requirement that covenant descendants marry within the family rather than among the Canaanites. It is this grief that drives Rebekah's plan for Jacob in ch.27.

tx.title: Third wife-sister narrative — textual consistency
tx.content: The third wife-sister episode is textually consistent across MT, SP, and LXX. As with chapters 12 and 20, no ancient witness attempts to harmonize or merge the three parallel accounts. The LXX renders mĕṣaḥēq (v. 8, 'caressing/laughing with') as παίζοντα, preserving the Isaac/laughter pun.
tx.note: The preservation of three similar narratives without scribal harmonization is a strong argument for the conservative character of the textual transmission process.

positions.name: Passive/secondary figure
positions.argument: Isaac is the most passive of the patriarchs — he is nearly sacrificed, his wife is chosen for him, his sons deceive him, he is never called by God to go anywhere. He inherits and passes on the covenant but rarely acts.

positions.name: Sustained covenant faithfulness
positions.argument: Isaac's passivity may reflect faithfulness: he stays where God sends him (26:2-3), he digs wells, he makes peace. His lack of dramatic narrative reflects steady covenant obedience rather than the crises of Abraham and Jacob.
